coredata.dc:title: Ultra-narrow bandwidth red-emission carbon quantum dots and their bio-imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Peiguang
Song, Yuming
Tang, Ju
Nie, Zhifeng
Chang, Jiawei
Chen, Qiuyuan
He, Yunfei
Guo, Tingting
Zhang, Jin
Wang, Hai
coredata.dc:description: One-step solvothermal method was used to synthesize red fluorescent carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) with ultra-narrow bandwidth emission by using the titanyl-phthalocyanine (TiOPc) as the reaction precursor and ethanol as the organic solvent. We characterized the structural and optical properties of the R-CQDs by a variety of spectroscopic methods such as photoluminescence. Our studies have shown that the photoluminescence properties of R-CQDs are independent of the excitation wavelength. The strongest emission peak is at 674 nm, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) is only 23.2 nm, and the quantum yield (QY) is 7.54%. In addition, we also studied the application of the R-CQDs as a potential diagnostic marker. It was observed that the R-CQDs could enter the aloe tissue. The R- CQDs reported here have a narrow width and excellent QY characteristics, which can be attributed to the surface and edge C = O, C = N groups and Ti doping in the R-CQDs. Such R-CQDs can be widely used in the fields of display device, cell labelling and bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Ultra-narrow bandwidth
Quantum yield
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphology and structure of the R-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the R-CQDs (inset: the HR-TEM image of the R-CQDs). (b) The particle-size distribution measured by TEM. (c) The XRD pattern of the R-CQDs.
(a) XPS spectrum and high-resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s and (e) Ti2p spectra of the R-CQDs. (f) FT-IR spectrum of R-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the R-CQDs. (b)UV–Vis absorption (black) and PL spectra (red and blue) for the R-CQDs dispersed in ethanol solution. (c) PL emission map for R-CQDs dispersed in ethanol solution, inset: the photograph of the CQDs under natural light (left) and 365 nm irradiation (right). (d) Time-resolved PL spectra of R- CQDs at 380 nm as a function of irradiation time.
Confocal fluorescence images of the R-CQDs in aloe tissue under 401 (a), 506 (b), and 559 nm (c) laser excitation, bright field (d), and merged images (e), (f).

head.ce:title: Ultra-narrow bandwidth red-emission carbon quantum dots and their bio-imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Yunnan Provincial Higher Education Institutions for Organic Optoelectronic Materials and Devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One-step solvothermal method was used to synthesize red fluorescent carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) with ultra-narrow bandwidth emission by using the titanyl-phthalocyanine (TiOPc) as the reaction precursor and ethanol as the organic solvent. We characterized the structural and optical properties of the R-CQDs by a variety of spectroscopic methods such as photoluminescence. Our studies have shown that the photoluminescence properties of R-CQDs are independent of the excitation wavelength. The strongest emission peak is at 674 nm, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) is only 23.2 nm, and the quantum yield (QY) is 7.54%. In addition, we also studied the application of the R-CQDs as a potential diagnostic marker. It was observed that the R-CQDs could enter the aloe tissue. The R- CQDs reported here have a narrow width and excellent QY characteristics, which can be attributed to the surface and edge C = O, C = N groups and Ti doping in the R-CQDs. Such R-CQDs can be widely used in the fields of display device, cell labelling and bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the advent of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in 2006, they have received much attention and rapidly became an emerging excellent fluorescent nanomaterial. The CQDs have high electron mobility, long thermal electron lifetime, extremely fast electron extraction speed, adjustable band gap, and strong steady-state fluorescence. The CQDs are cheap, of low toxicity, and bio-compatible, which can be processed in solutions. They have been considered as the ideal materials for the fabrication of efficient and inexpensive solar cells (SCs), light-emitting diodes and bio-imaging devices [1–6]. Hu et al. prepared red luminescence carbon dots through complex steps, but the quantum yield (QY) was only 6% [7]. Qu et al. reported an orange luminescent carbon dots synthesized by using citric acid and urea as precursors, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) as solvent and modified with sodium hydroxide. The QY was as high as 46%, but the emission peak of the CDs was at 580 nm [8]. Using phloroglucinol as the reaction precursor, concentrated sulfuric acid as the catalyst and ethanol as the solvent, Yuan et al. synthesized triangular red emission CQDs with 30 nm FWHM by one-step solvothermal method, and applied it for light emitting diodes [9]. Furthermore, there have been few studies on the CQDs with excitation independent of wavelengths and narrow emission width in the long wavelength region, the fluorescence emission wavelength of CQDs is mostly concentrated in the band of 410–600 nm [10]. However, The PL emission ranging across the entire region of the visible spectrum is a key to realize CQDs reach the requirements for the practical application. Therefore, the design and preparation of high-purity CQDs with Long wavelength emission (such as red emitting) and a FWHM of less than 40 nm are important and meaningful for its application, especially in the display field [6,11–16].

In this work, the red fluorescent CQD (R-CQDs) with ultra-narrow band emission were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method with titanyl phthalocyanine (TiOPc) as the reaction precursor and ethanol as the organic solvent. The prepared R-CQDs have a photoluminescence (PL) peak at 674 nm with an absolute QY of 7.54%. Meanwhile, they show an emission independent of excitation with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 23.2 nm. The preparation method of the R-CQDs is very simple without the need of any catalysts such as strong acid. In addition, it is very important that we successfully applied it to aloe tissue imaging.

The R-CQDs were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method. TiOPc (30 mg) was dissolved in ethanol (60 mL) and sonicated for 30 min. The precursor solution was then transferred to a 100 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon) lined autoclave. We then tightened the reaction kettle and put it in the oven for 6 h at 180 °C. After the reaction was completed and the reactor was cooled to the room temperature naturally, the impurities of large particles were filtered out by 220 nm filter head. Finally, the R-CQDs were obtained by silica gel column chromatography with ethanol as eluent.

The morphologies and nanostructure of the R-CQDs were investigated by using transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM2100, Japan) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD) using Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) (Rigaku TTR-III, Japan). The absorption spectra for the R-CQDs was received from the ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra and quantum yield of the R-CQDs were measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer spectrometer (FS5, Edinburgh, UK). The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the R-CQDs was obtained by infra-red spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS 10, Thermo Scientific, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were gained by a K-Alpha + photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo fisher Scientific, USA) with Al Kα at 1486.6 eV. The cell images were captured by using a Confocal Laser Scanning Microscopy (FV10i, Olympus, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and structure of the R-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We performed the particle-size statistics of the R-CQDs by Image J software, which is showed in Fig. 1 (a) and (b). All the R-CQDs samples have a particle size distribution of approximately 2.1–4.9 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 3.4 nm. The lattice spacing measured by the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) was 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite, indicating that the R-CQDs have a graphite-like structure [17]. The XRD spectra of the R-CQDs (Fig. 2 (c)) possess a distinct diffraction peak at approximately 25.14° due to the (002) plane of graphite [18].

XPS and FT-IR spectroscopies are used to further analyzed the chemical structure of R- CQDs. In Fig. 2(a), the XPS full spectrum shows four peaks at 288.55, 399.20, 531.75 and 457.98 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and Ti2p, and their atomic ratios are respectively 86.89%, 3.39%, 9.62% and 0.1%, indicating that R-CQDs consist of C, N, O and Ti elements. As shown in Fig. 2(b)–(e), the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s are divided into three peaks at 284.90, 286.18, and 288.18 eV, corresponding to C–C/C = C, C–O/C–N and CO/CN, respectively [19,20]. N1s has three peaks at 398.18, 398.98 and 399.88 eV, corresponding to pyrrole N, pyridine N and graphite N, respectively. O1s has three peaks at 530.48, 531.79 and 532.88 eV, corresponding to Ti–O, C = O and C–O, respectively [21,22]. Ti2p is separated to Ti2P 3/2 peak at 458.08 eV and Ti2P1/2 peak at 463.48 eV [23,24]. Fig. 2(f) shows the FT-IR spectrum of R-CQDs. The stretching vibrations of N–H, C–H and C = C/CN are located at 3344 cm−1, 2974 cm−1 and 2901 cm− 1 and 1655 cm−1, respectively. The stretching vibrations of the lactone structure of C = O and C–O–C are located at 1250 cm−1 and 1051 cm−1, respectively. The vibration of C–N at 1394 cm−1 contributes to the amide group. The out-of-plane bending of aromatic C–H is at 881 cm−1 and 646 cm−1. Finally, there is a weak absorption peak near 433 cm−1, which corresponds to the Ti–O stretching vibration [25,26]. The results show that there are CO, CN groups on the surface and edge of R-CQDs, and the Ti element (TiOPc as precursor).

As shown in Fig. 3 , we studied the optical properties of R-CQDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of R-CQDs shows five distinct absorption peaks at about 221, 282, 338, 603 and 667 nm, respectively. The strong absorption peak at 221 and 282 nm is attributed to the π→π* transition of aromatic C = C bond [27]. The absorption peaks at 338, 603, and 667 nm are weak due to the n→π* transitions with the aromatic C = O or C–N/C = N structures [27,28]. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the emission mapping spectrum of R-CQDs exhibits good excitation-energy independence at an excitation wavelength of 300–420 nm, where in the strongest emission peak of R-CQDs at the optimum excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The results in Fig. 3(b) also illustrate this fact, the blue line is the excitation spectrum of the R-CQDs in Fig. 3(b). Although the intensity of excitation peak at 340 nm and 600 nm appear to be consistent, but the fluorescence intensity of R- CQDs is essentially different at 340 nm and 600 nm, respectively. As shown in Figure S1, the intensity of excitation peak at 340 nm is greater than that at ∼600 nm. Furthermore, in principle, electrons in the excited state often go through relaxation, release phonons (thermal motion), and then return to the ground state, resulting in Stokes shift. At this time, the emission peak wavelength generated is longer than the excitation wavelength, forming a one-to-one mirror image relationship. However, if the electron in the excited state directly returns to the ground state without relaxation, the excitation peak and emission peak overlap. Thus, the emission peak of R-CQDs at 674 nm overlaps with its absorption peak at 667 nm.

As shown in Figure S2, we measured the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 7.54% by a fluorescence spectrophotometer spectrometer (FS5 integrating sphere). As shown in Figure S3, The R-CQDs obtained by Origin software (Microcal Software, Northhampton, MA) [29] have a FWHW of 23.2 nm. As for our best knowledge, this FWHM is very narrow as compared with the current literature, leading to excellent optical absorption properties. We speculate that their excellent properties are attributed to the size distribution and the surface/defect state of R-CQDs. Previous studies have shown that the high carboxyl group content leads to a high efficiency and the uniform size distribution results in a narrow bandwidth [30]. In addition, the CQDs with an ultra-narrow FWHM in long wavelength emissive, which shows obvious long absorption wavelengths [31], indicates the high conjugation of CQDs [30]. Furthermore, R-CQDs also have a large Stokes shift, which will facilitate their application in bio-imaging. As shown in Fig. 3(d), time resolution fluorescence spectra shows that the fluorescence lifetime of R-CQDs is 3.9 ns, and the fluorescence decay curve satisfies the single exponential decay curve.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the confocal micrographs show different colors of emission in a part of aloe tissue under the 401, 506 and 559 nm laser excitation, demonstrating the R-CQDs could potentially be employed in bio-labeling through either endocytosis or diffusion process. It is worthy to note from these figures that the emissions locate nearly at the whole cellular regions, suggesting the R-CQDs can enter into aloe tissue. This outstanding properties are due to their nanometer particle size. Furthermore, during the PL measurement, the influence of UV exposure on the PL intensity of the R-CQDs had been examined. The PL intensity does not vary under continuous UV irradiation for hours for R-CQDs in ethanol. This indicate that the R-CQDs prepared are quite stable and would have good photostability and long-lived resistance of photo-bleaching. The long-lived resistance of photo-bleaching for R-CQDs, in combination with their good photonic stability, makes them the perfect alternatives to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots for fluorescent bio-imaging.

We used a one-step solvothermal method to synthesize R-CQDs with an ultra-narrow emission bandwidth using TiOPc as the reaction precursor and ethanol as the organic solvent. The prepared R-CQDs showed good excitation-energy-independent PL behavior, the absolute QY of R-CQDs was determined to be 7.54%, and the FWHM of R-CQD was 23.2 nm. The excellent properties of R-CQDs are attributed to the synergistic effect of R- CQDs surface and edge CO, CN group and Ti doping. In addition, R-CQDs have a nanometer particle size, which can enter into the aloe tissue. In the future, it is necessary to synthesize high quantum yield and ultra-narrow bandwidth emission for the other colors such as blue, and green CQDs. These CQDs can be widely used in bio-imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge Prof. Hao Gong (National University of Singapore) and Prof. Jing Liu (Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, CAS & Tsinghua University) for helpful discussions. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62164006, 11364045, 11564023, 11664044 and 51904137), Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (grant No. 202101AS070036), the Department of Science and Technology of Yunnan Province (Approval No. 2016FC001 and 2019FD044), Yunnan local university Joint special funds for basic research (2017FH001-007 and 2018FH001-017), Scientific Research Fund of Yunnan Education Department (2019J0563, 2019J0564, 2020Y0464, 2021Y704, and 2021Y713) and Yunnan University (Approval No. 2016MS14).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.P. Sun, B. Zhou, Y. Lin, W. Wang, K. Fernando, Quantum-Sized Carbon Dots for Bright and Colorful Photoluminescence, Journal of the American Chemical Society, 128 (2006) 7756-7757.

Nano Today
